questions.question: A service does `using var http = new HttpClient();` on every request and is called thousands of times per minute. What failure mode does this most directly cause?
options: Socket exhaustion â€” disposed sockets sit in `TIME_WAIT` until ephemeral ports run out and `SocketException` is thrown
An immediate `OutOfMemoryException` from allocating too many client objects
Stale DNS â€” the client keeps hitting a dead IP after a failover
Thread-pool starvation from blocking the request threads
questions.explanation: Each `new HttpClient()` opens its own connection pool; disposing it strands the underlying TCP socket in `TIME_WAIT` for ~4 minutes, so under load you exhaust ephemeral ports and get `SocketException`. Stale DNS is the symptom of the *opposite* mistake â€” one static client forever.

questions.question: Why is keeping one static `HttpClient` alive for the lifetime of the process *also* wrong, even though it fixes socket exhaustion?
options: It pins the connection so it never re-resolves DNS, and keeps hitting a dead IP after a failover or scale event
It is not thread-safe, so concurrent requests corrupt each other's headers
Static fields are garbage-collected unpredictably, so the client may vanish mid-request
It disables HTTP keep-alive, forcing a new TCP handshake on every call
questions.explanation: A single long-lived client reuses sockets but pins the connection, so it never picks up DNS changes (failover, blue-green, scaling) and keeps hitting the dead IP. `HttpClient` *is* thread-safe for concurrent requests, so that distractor is false.

questions.question: How does `IHttpClientFactory` give you both socket reuse and DNS freshness at the same time?
options: It reuses the pooled `HttpMessageHandler` (sharing sockets) but rotates it on a schedule (default 2 minutes) so a fresh handler periodically re-resolves DNS
It opens a brand-new socket per request but caches the DNS lookup for 2 minutes
It keeps one handler forever and runs a background DNS-refresh thread
It disposes and recreates the `HttpClient` on every call while keeping the socket open
questions.explanation: The factory pools and reuses the underlying handler for socket efficiency, but rotates that handler (default 2 minutes via `SetHandlerLifetime`) so a fresh handler eventually re-resolves DNS. It does not open a socket per request.

questions.question: The default `HttpMessageHandler` lifetime used by `IHttpClientFactory` is 2 minutes, and it can be changed with `SetHandlerLifetime`.
questions.explanation: The default handler lifetime is 2 minutes, configurable via `SetHandlerLifetime` (e.g. `TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5)`). The handler lifetime â€” not the client lifetime â€” governs socket/DNS behavior.

questions.question: Which package is the successor to the deprecated `Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Polly`, and what does it wrap?
options: `Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience`, which wraps Polly v8
`Polly.Extensions.Http`, which wraps Polly v7
`Microsoft.AspNetCore.Resilience`, which wraps the BCL retry handler
`System.Net.Http.Resilience`, which wraps a custom retry engine
questions.explanation: `Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience` replaces the deprecated `Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Polly`; it wraps Polly v8 and plugs a resilience pipeline into the handler chain as a delegating handler via `AddStandardResilienceHandler()`.

questions.question: What is the correct outermost-to-innermost order of the five strategies in `AddStandardResilienceHandler()`?
options: Rate limiter â†’ total timeout â†’ retry â†’ circuit breaker â†’ per-attempt timeout
Total timeout â†’ rate limiter â†’ circuit breaker â†’ retry â†’ per-attempt timeout
Retry â†’ rate limiter â†’ total timeout â†’ per-attempt timeout â†’ circuit breaker
Circuit breaker â†’ rate limiter â†’ retry â†’ total timeout â†’ per-attempt timeout
questions.explanation: The standard pipeline chains, outermost to innermost: rate limiter, total timeout (covers all retries), retry, circuit breaker, then per-attempt timeout. The retry loop sits inside the total-timeout budget and outside the per-attempt timeout.

questions.question: Using `AddStandardResilienceHandler()` defaults â€” per-attempt timeout 10s, retry 3Ă—, total timeout 30s â€” a dependency takes 12s on every call. What does the caller ultimately observe?
options: Each attempt hits the 10s per-attempt timeout and is retried, but the 30s total timeout caps the operation, so after a few attempts the whole call fails with a timeout
The first call succeeds at 12s because the per-attempt timeout only applies to retries
The call fails immediately with `BrokenCircuitException` since 12s exceeds 10s
The call retries forever because the per-attempt timeout resets the total budget each time
questions.explanation: Each 12s attempt trips the 10s per-attempt timeout and is retried, but the 30s total timeout â€” sitting outside the retry â€” caps the whole operation, so after roughly the first few attempts the budget is exhausted and the call fails with a timeout. The total timeout is what stops infinite waiting.

questions.question: A `PaymentClient` POSTs a charge and is registered with a bare `AddStandardResilienceHandler()`. What is the risk and the correct fix?
options: The handler retries all HTTP methods by default, so a transient failure can double-charge â€” fix with `o.Retry.DisableForUnsafeHttpMethods()` and/or an idempotency key
POST is never retried by default, so there is no risk and no change is needed
The risk is socket exhaustion from POST bodies â€” fix by raising the handler lifetime
The circuit breaker will refuse all POSTs â€” fix by lowering `FailureRatio`
questions.explanation: The standard handler retries *all* methods by default, so a transient failure on a POST can duplicate the write (double-charge). Disable retries for unsafe methods with `DisableForUnsafeHttpMethods()` and/or send an idempotency key so the gateway de-dupes.

questions.question: A teammate writes `Policy.Handle<HttpRequestException>().WaitAndRetryAsync(...)` and it won't compile against the current package. Why, and what is the v8 equivalent?
options: That fluent API is Polly v7, removed in v8 â€” use `new ResiliencePipelineBuilder().AddRetry(new RetryStrategyOptions { ... }).Build()` and `ExecuteAsync`
The method is async-only now â€” add `.ConfigureAwait(false)` to make it compile
`Policy` moved namespaces â€” add `using Polly.Legacy;` and it compiles unchanged
v8 renamed it to `Policy.Handle<>().Retry(...)` with no wait variant
questions.explanation: Polly v8 removed v7's fluent `Policy.Handle<>().WaitAndRetry(...)` API. The current model builds a reusable, thread-safe `ResiliencePipeline` via `ResiliencePipelineBuilder` + strategy options + `Build()`, executed with `ExecuteAsync`.

questions.question: In a hand-built Polly v8 pipeline, which strategy executes outermost, given the order strategies are added to the builder?
options: Fallback â€” the first strategy added is outermost and wraps the rest, so it catches whatever escapes the inner strategies
Timeout â€” the last strategy added runs first as the outermost guard
Circuit breaker â€” breakers always run before any other strategy regardless of order
Retry â€” retries are always promoted to the outermost position by the builder
questions.explanation: The first strategy added is outermost; strategies nest like middleware. Here fallback wraps retry, which wraps the circuit breaker, which wraps the timeout â€” so the per-attempt timeout is what retry re-issues and fallback catches whatever escapes.

questions.question: When a Polly v8 timeout strategy fires, which exception is thrown â€” and which `ShouldHandle` predicate must catch it?
options: `TimeoutRejectedException` â€” not the BCL `TimeoutException`, so predicates must handle that specific type
`TimeoutException` from the BCL, so catching `System.TimeoutException` is correct
`OperationCanceledException`, because timeouts cancel the underlying token
`BrokenCircuitException`, since a timeout is treated as a breaker trip
questions.explanation: Polly's timeout strategy throws `TimeoutRejectedException`, not the BCL `TimeoutException`. A `ShouldHandle` predicate that expects `System.TimeoutException` will silently miss it.

questions.question: Setting `UseJitter = true` on a retry strategy randomizes each backoff delay so that many clients don't all retry on the same tick, preventing a synchronized retry storm.
questions.explanation: Exponential backoff spaces attempts apart, but without jitter thousands of clients retry on the same tick (thundering herd). `UseJitter = true` desynchronizes them, which is the single toggle that breaks up the retry storm.

questions.question: Which statements about Polly v8's circuit breaker trigger and behavior are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It opens only when the failure *ratio* exceeds the threshold AND at least `MinimumThroughput` calls occurred within the sampling window
While Open, calls throw `BrokenCircuitException` immediately with no network round-trip
After `BreakDuration` elapses it goes Half-Open and lets one trial call decide whether to close or re-open
It opens as soon as any single call fails, regardless of call volume
While Open it keeps issuing calls but logs each failure until `BreakDuration` elapses
questions.explanation: The v8 breaker uses a sampling window: it opens only when the failure ratio is exceeded *and* `MinimumThroughput` was met, then fails fast with `BrokenCircuitException` while Open, and probes once in Half-Open after `BreakDuration`. It does not trip on a single failure, nor does it keep calling while Open.

questions.question: Where in a resilience pipeline should the fallback strategy sit, and why?
options: Outermost, so it catches everything the inner strategies (retry, breaker, timeout) ultimately surface and can substitute a safe default
Innermost, so it runs before the network call and short-circuits early
Immediately after retry but before the circuit breaker, so it only catches retry exhaustion
It must not be combined with a circuit breaker, since both handle failures
questions.explanation: Fallback belongs outermost so it catches everything the inner strategies surface (including `BrokenCircuitException`) and supplies a substitute outcome â€” cached data, a default object â€” letting the caller degrade gracefully. It composes fine with a breaker.

questions.question: Your team wants to add custom retry behavior on top of `AddStandardResilienceHandler()` for one client. What is the correct guidance?
options: Add exactly one resilience handler per client; for a non-standard composition use `AddResilienceHandler("name", builder => ...)` and build the pipeline yourself rather than stacking handlers
Call `AddStandardResilienceHandler()` twice â€” the second call merges its options into the first
Stack a second `AddStandardResilienceHandler()` and lower its retry count so the two cooperate
Wrap the client in another typed client that also adds a standard handler, to layer the pipelines
questions.explanation: The rule is exactly one resilience handler per client â€” stacking handlers multiplies retries and timeouts unpredictably. For a custom composition, drop the standard handler and use `AddResilienceHandler("name", builder => ...)` to build the pipeline yourself.

questions.question: Stashing the `HttpClient` injected into a typed client in a static field or a singleton is safe because the factory already pools the handler behind it.
questions.explanation: Capturing the injected `HttpClient` statically or in a singleton re-pins the handler and defeats the rotation that keeps DNS fresh. The typed client is transient *precisely* so each use gets a fresh client over a rotating pooled handler â€” let the factory hand you a new one.

questions.question: Which statements about using Polly v8 resilience pipelines for non-HTTP work and for testing are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: A `ResiliencePipeline` wraps any delegate, so the same retry/breaker patterns apply to DB calls, queue publishes, and gRPC
Shared named pipelines can be registered with `AddResiliencePipeline` and resolved via `ResiliencePipelineProvider<TKey>` so they're built once
`ResiliencePipeline.Empty` is a no-op pipeline useful for bypassing resilience in tests
Resilience pipelines only function inside the HTTP handler chain and cannot wrap a `SaveChangesAsync` call
To test retry behavior you must make real network calls; in-memory `DelegatingHandler` stubs cannot drive the retry path
questions.explanation: A `ResiliencePipeline` is not HTTP-specific â€” it wraps any delegate (DB, queue, gRPC), shared pipelines register via `AddResiliencePipeline` and resolve through `ResiliencePipelineProvider<TKey>`, and `ResiliencePipeline.Empty` is the no-op for tests. You can absolutely unit-test the strategy with a `DelegatingHandler` stub that returns 503 a fixed number of times, no real network needed.

questions.question: Your microservice's typed client retries 3Ă—, and the service mesh (Envoy/Istio) in front of the dependency *also* retries. What is the senior concern and the correct response?
options: Retries multiply across layers (client Ă— mesh), amplifying load on a struggling dependency â€” pick one layer to own retries, not both
The two retry layers automatically deduplicate, so the effective retry count stays 3 and no action is needed
The mesh retry overrides the client retry, so the client config is silently ignored and can be left as-is
Client retries are always preferred over mesh retries, so disable mesh retries and quadruple the client count
questions.explanation: If both the client and the mesh/gateway retry, attempts multiply (e.g. 3Ă—3), turning one slow service into a system-wide brownout. The fix is to pick a single layer to own retries rather than stacking them â€” there is no automatic dedup or override.
